refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall automatically derive performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context and operational parameters, record them in a traceable baseline, and maintain a managed history of all requirement updates and calibration changes with full audit trail.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on 'method' and lacked explicit traceability and history management. Refinement adds specificity: (1) automatic derivation from mission context, (2) explicit baseline recording, (3) managed history with audit trail. This aligns with acceptance criteria demanding documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baselines/calibration updates. High safety criticality demands explicit safety margin derivation and audit capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (a) performance requirements are automatically computed from worksite mission inputs (e.g., terrain, payload, cycle count); (b) baseline is recorded with timestamp, source, and derivation method; (c) history log captures all updates with reason, timestamp, and approver; (d) traceability chain from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline → history is complete and auditable; (e) safety margins are explicitly calculated and documented.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) from Site Manager input.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-driven algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record derived performance requirements as a baseline with metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement baselines and calibration updates with full audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability reporting linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baselines → history.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine – A dedicated software module that ingests worksite mission parameters, applies deterministic algorithms (physics-based cycle time estimation, energy models, availability calculations), and outputs a structured performance requirement set. Baseline and history managed in a versioned configuration database with audit logging.

DP-B: Distributed Derivation with Consensus – Performance requirements derived collaboratively across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, each contributing domain-specific estimates (e.g., Vehicle provides mechanical cycle time, Safety Services provides safety margin). Results merged and recorded centrally.

DP-C: Template-Based Requirement Mapping – Pre-defined requirement templates matched against worksite mission context via lookup tables and heuristics. Minimal computation; fast baseline generation. History tracked via configuration management system.

DP-D: Hybrid Derivation with Real-Time Calibration – Initial baseline derived from mission context using DP-A logic, then continuously refined using telemetry feedback from Vehicle and Teleoperation. Baselines updated incrementally with calibration deltas.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine with Versioned Configuration Database and Audit Logging

potential_sources: DP-A requires inputs from worksite mission context (Site Manager, Safety Manager) – this is a functional dependency, not a design coupling. The derivation engine is logically independent of Vehicle and Teleoperation operation.
DP-A outputs performance targets that constrain Vehicle and Teleoperation behavior – this is a one-way information flow (DP-A → downstream), not a bidirectional coupling.
Audit logging and baseline versioning are standard practices; no novel coupling introduced.
Risk: If derivation algorithms are tightly coupled to specific vehicle models or teleoperation modes, maintainability suffers. Mitigation: algorithms parameterized by vehicle class and mission type.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits low coupling because it is a pure computation and data management layer. It consumes mission context and produces requirement artifacts; it does not depend on real-time vehicle state or teleoperation feedback for baseline derivation. This supports modularity and testability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite mission context is well-defined and available at system initialization (Site Manager provides terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints).
Assumption: Physics-based algorithms for cycle time, energy efficiency, and availability are sufficiently accurate for the NGHE domain.
Risk: If mission context is incomplete or ambiguous, derived requirements may be invalid. Mitigation: validation rules and user confirmation gates.
Risk: Algorithm tuning and calibration require domain expertise. Mitigation: parameterized models with documented tuning procedures.
Risk: High safety criticality demands that safety margin derivation is conservative and auditable. Mitigation: explicit safety factor tables, documented assumptions, and independent review.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-A is sound. Recommend adding explicit specification of: (1) algorithm inputs and outputs (schema), (2) safety margin calculation method (e.g., factor of safety tables), (3) baseline versioning and audit log format, (4) validation rules for mission context completeness.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary. It owns the Performance Derivation Engine (DP-A logic), the Baseline Configuration Database, and the Audit Logging subsystem. It receives mission context inputs from Site Manager and Safety Manager, computes and records performance requirements, and publishes requirement baselines to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules via the Tasking interface.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution publishes derived performance requirements (cycle time targets, energy efficiency targets, availability targets, safety margins) to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules. Format: structured requirement set with baseline version, timestamp, and traceability metadata.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution may optionally ingest telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation to validate derived requirements against actual performance (post-baseline). Used for calibration history updates and trend analysis, not for initial baseline derivation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives safety alerts and constraints from Safety Services. These inform safety margin calculations and may trigger requirement re-derivation if safety context changes.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution communicates with Site Manager and Safety Manager via Network Link to receive mission context inputs and publish requirement baselines and audit reports.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of performance requirement derivation, baseline management, and audit logging. The entire DP-A logic (derivation engine, configuration database, audit subsystem) fits cleanly within this boundary. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces are mission context inputs and requirement outputs.

Vehicle module is responsible for mechanical and operational behavior, not for system-level performance requirement derivation. If DP-A logic were embedded in Vehicle, it would be tightly coupled to vehicle-specific models and difficult to reuse across different vehicle types or mission contexts. High diffusion risk.

Teleoperation module handles remote operation and control, not performance requirement derivation. Embedding DP-A in Teleoperation would create unnecessary coupling between tele-operation control logic and system-level performance planning. High diffusion risk.

Safety Services is responsible for safety monitoring and alerts, not for deriving performance requirements from mission context. While Safety Services contributes safety constraints to the derivation process, it should not own the derivation engine itself. Ownership would blur the boundary between safety constraint provision and system-level performance planning.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Performance Derivation Engine (DP-A) is best contained within the NGHE Solution module because: (1) it is a system-level function that integrates mission context from multiple stakeholders (Site Manager, Safety Manager) and produces system-wide performance targets; (2) it is logically independent of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services operation; (3) it requires a centralized, auditable baseline and history, which is naturally managed at the system level; (4) containment within NGHE Solution minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution module is not yet fully defined or has unclear boundaries, assignment may be premature. Mitigation: confirm NGHE Solution scope and ownership structure with System Engineer and Project Manager.

Risk: If mission context inputs are not reliably available at system initialization, the derivation engine may fail or produce invalid baselines. Mitigation: implement validation gates and user confirmation workflows.

Risk: If performance requirement baselines are not actively maintained and updated, they may become stale and lose traceability. Mitigation: establish clear ownership and update procedures; integrate with configuration management system.
Risk: Audit logging and versioning add complexity and storage overhead. Mitigation: define retention policies and archive procedures; use efficient data structures.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assignment of DP-A to NGHE Solution is sound. Recommend: (1) explicitly define the NGHE Solution module boundary and ownership in the system architecture document; (2) specify the interface contracts for mission context inputs (from Site Manager, Safety Manager) and requirement outputs (to Vehicle, Teleoperation); (3) document the baseline versioning and audit logging schema; (4) establish configuration management procedures for baseline updates and calibration changes.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (Centralized Performance Derivation Engine with Versioned Configuration Database and Audit Logging) directly addresses the refined FR-NGHE-005. The DP specifies: (1) automatic derivation of performance requirements from mission context (addresses 'derive'), (2) recording in a traceable baseline (addresses 'record'), (3) managed history with audit trail (addresses 'manage'). The DP is logically sound, low-coupling, and feasible. No design contradictions or missing elements identified.

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: DP-A is contained within NGHE Solution with clear external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No logic is scattered across multiple modules.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner. The module boundary is clear: NGHE Solution owns the derivation engine, configuration database, and audit subsystem. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are consumers of the derived requirements, not owners. Ownership is unambiguous.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible. Test cases can be designed to: (1) provide worksite mission context inputs; (2) execute the derivation engine; (3) confirm that performance requirements are computed correctly; (4) verify that baselines are recorded with complete metadata; (5) validate that history logs capture all updates with audit trail; (6) confirm traceability from worksite input to derived requirement to baseline to history. All verification activities are within the scope of NGHE Solution module testing.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear. CR-NGHE-005 is allocated to NGHE Solution. Sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5) are allocated to specific functional areas within NGHE Solution (mission context ingestion, requirement computation, baseline recording, history management, traceability reporting). No ambiguity or overlap with other modules.

recommended_revisions: Confirm that NGHE Solution module is fully defined and has clear ownership and scope boundaries.
Document the interface contracts for mission context inputs (from Site Manager, Safety Manager) and requirement outputs (to Vehicle, Teleoperation) in the system architecture document.
Specify the performance requirement derivation algorithms (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) with documented assumptions and tuning parameters.
Define the baseline versioning and audit logging schema, including data retention and archive policies.
Establish configuration management procedures for baseline updates and calibration changes, with approval workflows and traceability.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-005 has been successfully refined to a clear, traceable FR with explicit sub-FRs. DP-A is sound and well-justified. IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate and low-risk. All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass. However, the refined FR is not yet atomic; it contains five sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5) that require further decomposition. Recommend proceeding to the next level of functional decomposition to address each sub-FR individually, ensuring that each is mapped to a specific DP and IM assignment. This will provide the detailed design clarity needed for implementation and verification.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) from Site Manager input.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-driven algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record derived performance requirements as a baseline with metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement baselines and calibration updates with full audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability reporting linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baselines → history.

assumptions: Worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) is well-defined and available at system initialization from Site Manager and Safety Manager.
Physics-based algorithms for cycle time, energy efficiency, and availability estimation are sufficiently accurate for the NGHE domain.
NGHE Solution module is fully defined with clear ownership and scope boundaries.
Performance requirement baselines are actively maintained and updated throughout the system lifecycle.
Audit logging and versioning infrastructure is available and integrated with configuration management.
open_risks: If mission context is incomplete or ambiguous, derived requirements may be invalid. Mitigation: implement validation rules and user confirmation gates.
If algorithm tuning and calibration require extensive domain expertise, deployment and maintenance may be challenging. Mitigation: parameterized models with documented tuning procedures and training.
High safety criticality demands that safety margin derivation is conservative and auditable. Mitigation: explicit safety factor tables, documented assumptions, and independent review.

If baseline versioning and audit logging are not properly implemented, traceability and compliance may be compromised. Mitigation: establish clear configuration management procedures and regular audits.
evidence_needed: Detailed specification of performance requirement derivation algorithms (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) with documented assumptions and tuning parameters.
Baseline versioning and audit logging schema, including data retention and archive policies.
Configuration management procedures for baseline updates and calibration changes, with approval workflows and traceability.
Test cases and verification procedures for each sub-FR (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5).
Interface specifications for mission context inputs (from Site Manager, Safety Manager) and requirement outputs (to Vehicle, Teleoperation).
Safety analysis and justification for safety margin calculation methods, with independent review and approval.
